---
title: "shinygen"
subtitle: "Generate, evaluate, and refine Shiny apps using LLM agents in Docker sandboxes"
jupyter: shinygen
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: flatly
      dark: darkly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    smooth-scroll: true
    anchor-sections: true
    fontsize: 1.05em
    mainfont: "Inter, system-ui, sans-serif"
    highlight-style: github
    css: styles.css
---

::: {.lead}
**shinygen** turns a text prompt into a production-quality [Shiny](https://shiny.posit.co) dashboard — generated, screenshot-tested, and quality-scored — all inside an isolated Docker sandbox.
:::

---

## {{< fa bolt >}} Quick Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/karangathe/shinygen.git
cd shinygen
pip install -e .
```

For screenshot-based evaluation, install the optional extra:

```bash
pip install -e ".[screenshot]"
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Docker images with Shiny, Playwright, and Chromium are pulled automatically on first run.
:::

---

## {{< fa diagram-project >}} Architecture

The current loop is **generate inside a fresh sandbox → optionally self-evaluate visually → optionally judge externally → refine**.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart TD
  A[User Prompt + flags] --> B[shinygen CLI / API]
    B --> C["Pre-flight checks<br/>Docker + API key"]
    C --> D["Load framework defaults,<br/>data files, and skills"]
  D --> E[Iteration loop]

  subgraph F[Fresh Docker sandbox per iteration]
    direction TB
        G["Stage Docker context<br/>+ Inspect AI task"]
    H[LLM Agent]
    I[Generate app.py / app.R]
    J{--screenshot?}
        K["Run app on :8000<br/>inside sandbox"]
        L["screenshot_helper.py<br/>waits 7s and captures screenshot.png"]
        M["Agent reviews screenshot<br/>and refines in sandbox"]
        N["Scorer copies project files<br/>to results volume"]
    O[Eval log + usage rows]

    E --> G --> H --> I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K --> L --> M --> N
    J -- No --> N
    N --> O
  end

    O --> P["Extract code from results volume<br/>or eval log"]
    O --> Q["Copy agent_last_screenshot.png<br/>from results or eval attachment"]
  P --> R{--judge-model?}
  R -- Yes --> S[Run extracted app on host temp dir]
  S --> T[Host Playwright screenshot]
  T --> U["Judge panel<br/>(1+ external LLM judges)"]
  U --> Y["Average per-criterion scores<br/>+ concatenate rationales"]
  Y --> V{Score ≥ threshold?}
    V -- No --> W["Refinement prompt includes<br/>panel feedback + previous code"]
  W --> E
  V -- Yes --> X[Output directory]
  R -- No --> X

  Q --> X

  style A fill:#6c5ce7,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style B fill:#0984e3,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style C fill:#74b9ff,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style D fill:#636e72,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style F fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#dee2e6,stroke-width:2px
  style H fill:#00b894,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style I fill:#fdcb6e,color:#2d3436,stroke:none
  style Q fill:#00cec9,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style U fill:#e17055,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style Y fill:#d63031,color:#fff,stroke:none
  style X fill:#00b894,color:#fff,stroke:none
```

### How it works

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Step-by-step walkthrough (click to expand)

1. **You run a command** — provide a prompt, pick a model, optionally enable `--screenshot` and `--judge-model`.
2. **Pre-flight + setup** — shinygen verifies Docker/API access, resolves the framework and model, and loads bundled skills, custom skills, and any data files.
3. **Fresh sandbox generation** — each iteration stages a fresh Docker sandbox, installs agent skills in the agent's native home, and asks Claude Code or Codex CLI to generate `app.py` or `app.R`.
4. **Visual self-evaluation** — when screenshot mode is enabled, the agent runs the app inside the sandbox, uses `screenshot_helper.py`, waits 7 seconds before capture, and refines visually before submission.
5. **Extraction + optional judge** — shinygen extracts the app from the results volume or eval log, preserves the final in-agent screenshot as `agent_last_screenshot.png`, and, if `--judge-model` is set, runs the extracted app on the host for a separate Playwright screenshot and one or more external LLM judges. shinygen and the benchmark workflow currently default to a single visual judge, `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, since it is the strongest multimodal scorer available. Repeat the `--judge-model` flag (e.g. `--judge-model anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 --judge-model openai/gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`) to run a panel: shinygen averages per-criterion scores across judges and concatenates each judge's rationale into the refinement prompt.
6. **Final output** — the final app, screenshots, eval logs, and structured run summary are written to your output directory.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Iteration by Feedback

With `max_iterations > 1`, each round runs in a fresh sandbox. The refinement prompt carries forward judge feedback **and** the previous code, so the agent can improve incrementally rather than starting from scratch.

**Original prompt (iteration 1):**

```text
Create a Shiny dashboard for clinical trials with filters, a trend chart, and a summary table.
```

**Refinement prompt (iteration 2):**

```text
Create a Shiny dashboard for clinical trials with filters, a trend chart, and a summary table.

--- PREVIOUS CODE ---
<the full app.py from the previous iteration>

--- REFINEMENT (iteration 1) ---

A previous version of this app was evaluated and received the following scores.
Please improve the app to address the feedback:

  - Functionality: 7.0/10 — Filters work as expected.
  - Design: 5.5/10 — Layout is cramped and spacing is inconsistent.
  - Code Quality: 6.0/10 — Repeated logic in multiple places.
  - Ux: 6.2/10 — Table readability is weak.

Focus on improving the lowest-scoring areas while maintaining what already works well.
Produce the complete, improved app file.
```
:::

---

## {{< fa boxes-stacked >}} What You Get

```
my-dashboard/
├── app.py                  # Or app.R for Shiny for R
├── data.csv                # Your data file (if provided)
├── screenshot.png          # Host-side screenshot used by the external judge
├── agent_last_screenshot.png
├── eval_logs/
│   └── *.eval
└── run_summary.json        # Structured score, usage, and artifact metadata
```

The CLI prints a results summary:

```
Score: 8.25 / 10.00 (after 2 iterations)
Time:  45.2s total (38.1s generate, 7.1s judge)
Tokens: 12,340 input / 3,210 output
Cost:  $0.1842
```

---

## {{< fa star >}} Features

::: {.feature-grid}

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa robot >}} Multiple LLM Agents
Claude Code (Anthropic) and Codex CLI (OpenAI) — pick the model that fits your budget and quality needs.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa brands docker >}} Docker Sandboxes
Every generation runs in an isolated container via [Inspect AI](https://inspect.ai-safety-institute.org.uk/). No side effects on your host.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa brands python >}} {{< fa brands r-project >}} R and Python
Generate Shiny for Python (`app.py`) or Shiny for R (`app.R`) from the same CLI.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa camera-retro >}} Visual Self-Evaluation
The agent takes Playwright screenshots *inside* the sandbox, reviews them, and self-corrects layout/styling issues.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa scale-balanced >}} External LLM Judge
A separate model scores apps on functionality, design, code quality, and UX — then triggers refinement if needed.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa arrows-rotate >}} Iterative Refinement
Automatically re-generate until the quality threshold is met, up to `--max-iterations`.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa book >}} Skills Injection
Pass custom skill files into the agent sandbox to control coding style, component choices, and best practices.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa flask-vial >}} Control vs Treatment Arms
Toggle the bundled framework skill on or off with `--skills` / `--no-skills` to A/B test how much the skill actually moves quality scores. Each run records its `arm` (`skills` or `vanilla`) in `run_summary.json`.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa globe >}} Web Fetch
Enabled by default. Allows the agent to search the web during generation for up-to-date API docs and examples. Use `--no-web-fetch` to disable.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
#### {{< fa coins >}} Cost & Time Tracking
Token usage, dollar costs, and timing breakdowns reported per run.
:::

:::

---

## {{< fa terminal >}} Quick Start

### CLI

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Basic

```bash
# Generate a Shiny for Python app with Claude Sonnet
shinygen generate \
    --prompt "Create a sales dashboard with filters by region and product category" \
    --model claude-sonnet \
    --output ./my-dashboard
```

#### With Screenshot + Judge

```bash
# Screenshot-based quality evaluation and iteration
shinygen generate \
    --prompt "Create a clinical trials dashboard" \
    --model claude-opus \
    --output ./trials-app \
    --screenshot \
    --judge-model claude-sonnet \
    --max-iterations 5
```

#### Shiny for R

```bash
shinygen generate \
    --prompt "Create an interactive data explorer" \
    --model claude-sonnet \
    --framework shiny-r \
    --output ./r-app
```

#### With Data + Skills

```bash
shinygen generate \
    --prompt "Build a dashboard for this dataset" \
    --model gpt54 \
    --output ./my-app \
    --skills-dir ./my-skills/ \
    --csv-file ./sales.csv
```

`web_fetch` is enabled by default. Use `--no-web-fetch` to disable web search.

:::

### Python API

```python
import shinygen

result = shinygen.generate(
    prompt="Create a sales dashboard with regional filters",
    model="claude-sonnet",
    output_dir="./my-dashboard",
    framework="shiny-python",
    data_csv="./sales.csv",
    screenshot=True,
    judge_model="claude-sonnet",
    max_iterations=5,
)

print(result.app_dir)       # ./my-dashboard
print(result.score)          # 4.5
print(result.iterations)    # 2
```

### Batch Generation

Run the **same prompt across multiple models** (or different prompts with different settings) in a single command. Each job gets its own output directory.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### CLI

Create a JSON config file. Each object is one job — same keys as the Python API. You can mix models, frameworks, and prompts freely.

```{.json filename="batch.json"}
[
  {
    "prompt": "Create a clinical trials dashboard with enrollment trends and status filters",
    "model": "claude-sonnet",
    "output_dir": "./batch/sonnet",
    "csv_file": "./test_data_csv_files/trials_short_50.csv",
    "screenshot": true,
        "judge_model": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7",
    "max_iterations": 3
  },
  { "model": "gpt54",       "output_dir": "./batch/gpt54", "..." : "same prompt/settings" },
  { "model": "claude-opus",  "output_dir": "./batch/opus",  "..." : "same prompt/settings" }
]
```

Then run:

```bash
shinygen batch --config batch.json
```

Output:

```
Starting batch run with 3 job(s)...

Batch complete: 3 succeeded, 0 failed
  [job 1] ./batch/sonnet  score=8.50  iterations=2  passed=True
  [job 2] ./batch/gpt54   score=7.25  iterations=3  passed=True
  [job 3] ./batch/opus    score=9.00  iterations=1  passed=True
```

#### Python API

```python
import shinygen

results = shinygen.batch([
    {
        "prompt": "Clinical trials dashboard with filters and charts",
        "model": "claude-sonnet",
        "output_dir": "./batch/sonnet",
        "data_csv": "./test_data_csv_files/trials_short_50.csv",
        "screenshot": True,
        "judge_model": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7",
    },
    # Add more jobs with different models/prompts...
])

print(f"{results.succeeded} succeeded, {results.failed} failed")
for r in results.results:
    print(f"  {r.app_dir}  score={r.score:.2f}")
```

:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Batch tips

- Jobs run **sequentially** (no Docker resource contention).
- A failed job doesn't stop the rest.
- Use different `output_dir` values per job.
- Relative paths resolve from the config file's directory.
:::

---

## {{< fa flask-vial >}} Control vs Treatment Benchmarks

shinygen ships a built-in A/B harness for measuring how much the bundled framework skill (and any `--skills-dir` extras) actually improves generated apps. Every generation runs in one of two **arms**:

| Arm | Flag | What loads into the sandbox |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `skills` (treatment) | `--skills` (default) | Bundled `shiny-python-dashboard` / `shiny-r-dashboard` skill plus any `--skills-dir` files |
| `vanilla` (control) | `--no-skills` | No skill files at all — the agent sees only the prompt and tools |

Both arms keep web search enabled, so the only intentional difference is the skill payload. Each run writes its arm into `run_summary.json`:

```json
{
  "arm": "vanilla",
  "use_skills": false,
  "score": 6.75,
  "iterations": 1,
  "passed": false,
  "...": "..."
}
```

### Run a paired comparison locally

```bash
# Treatment: bundled skill loaded
shinygen generate \
    --prompt "$(cat prompt.txt)" \
    --model claude-sonnet \
    --output ./bench/skills \
    --csv-file ./test_data_csv_files/trials_short_50.csv \
    --screenshot --judge-model claude-sonnet

# Control: same everything, no skill
shinygen generate \
    --prompt "$(cat prompt.txt)" \
    --model claude-sonnet \
    --output ./bench/vanilla \
    --csv-file ./test_data_csv_files/trials_short_50.csv \
    --screenshot --judge-model claude-sonnet \
    --no-skills
```

Or from the Python API:

```python
import shinygen

common = dict(
    prompt=open("prompt.txt").read(),
    model="claude-sonnet",
    data_csv="./test_data_csv_files/trials_short_50.csv",
    screenshot=True,
    judge_model="claude-sonnet",
)

treatment = shinygen.generate(output_dir="./bench/skills",  use_skills=True,  **common)
control   = shinygen.generate(output_dir="./bench/vanilla", use_skills=False, **common)

print(f"skills:  {treatment.score:.2f}")
print(f"vanilla: {control.score:.2f}")
```

### Benchmark matrix in CI

The `run-benchmark-matrix.yml` GitHub Actions workflow expands the control/treatment axis automatically: every `(framework, model)` cell runs **twice** — once as `skills`, once as `vanilla` — producing paired output directories named `…/<idx>-<model>-skills/` and `…/<idx>-<model>-vanilla/`. Downstream review tooling (e.g. the `ed_operations_benchmark` report) reads `run_summary.json` to compute per-model treatment lift.

::: {.callout-tip}
## When to use `--no-skills`

- Measuring whether a new skill version actually helps before merging it.
- Producing fair baselines for blog posts or model comparisons.
- Diagnosing whether a regression came from the model or from the skill.

For day-to-day app generation, leave skills on — they're the default for a reason.
:::

---

## {{< fa coins >}} Value Scoring

When a judge model is enabled, `score` is now a **value-adjusted score** rather than raw quality alone. The raw panel score is preserved as `quality_score`; `value_score` and `score_breakdown` show the deductions applied for extra generation iterations and generation cost.

```json
{
    "score": 7.15,
    "quality_score": 7.90,
    "value_score": 7.15,
    "score_breakdown": {
        "quality_score": 7.90,
        "value_score": 7.15,
        "iteration_penalty": 0.35,
        "cost_penalty": 0.40,
        "generation_cost": 0.40,
        "cost_known": true
    }
}
```

The default formula is intentionally simple and auditable:

```text
value_score = quality_score - iteration_penalty - cost_penalty
iteration_penalty = 0.35 * each extra generation iteration
cost_penalty = $1 generation cost = 1 score point, capped at 2.5 points
```

Judge cost is still recorded in `usage`, but it does not penalize the generating model because it is benchmark overhead rather than app-generation spend.

---

## {{< fa globe >}} OpenCode Go Models

shinygen can route OpenCode Go's OpenAI-compatible models through Inspect's `openai-api` provider and the `mini_swe_agent` Inspect SWE solver. This is the recommended path for value benchmarking because model calls are still proxied through Inspect, so eval logs, token usage, model usage, and the skills-vs-vanilla arm comparison remain intact.

Set your subscription key after signing up for OpenCode Go:

```bash
export OPENCODE_GO_API_KEY="sk-..."
```

Then run any supported OpenCode Go alias:

```bash
shinygen generate \
        --prompt "Build a polished dashboard for this dataset" \
        --model opencode-go/kimi-k2.6 \
        --csv-file ./test_data_csv_files/airbnb-asheville-short.csv \
        --screenshot \
        --judge-model anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
```

The alias `opencode-go/kimi-k2.6` resolves to `openai-api/opencode-go/kimi-k2.6`, and shinygen automatically sets `OPENCODE_GO_BASE_URL=https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1`. Supported aliases include `glm-5.1`, `glm-5`, `kimi-k2.5`, `kimi-k2.6`, `deepseek-v4-pro`, `deepseek-v4-flash`, `mimo-v2-pro`, `mimo-v2-omni`, `mimo-v2.5-pro`, `mimo-v2.5`, `qwen3.6-plus`, and `qwen3.5-plus`.

For a local value benchmark template that compares frontier US models and OpenCode Go models across both `skills` and `vanilla` arms, start from `batch-opencode-go.json`.

::: {.callout-note}
## About the OpenCode SDK and MiniMax models

OpenCode's TypeScript SDK controls an OpenCode server/session, which is useful for building a native OpenCode runner later. For this benchmark, using the SDK or `opencode run` directly would move model calls outside Inspect unless we also parse OpenCode session stats, so the first implementation uses Inspect's model bridge instead.

OpenCode Go currently exposes MiniMax M2.5 and M2.7 on an Anthropic-style `/messages` endpoint, while the implemented Inspect path targets the OpenAI-compatible `/chat/completions` models. Testing MiniMax through OpenCode Go should use a future native OpenCode CLI/SDK solver or a dedicated Inspect model extension so usage and cost stay measurable.
:::

---

## {{< fa microchip >}} Supported Models

```{python}
#| echo: false
from great_tables import GT, md
import polars as pl

models = pl.DataFrame({
    "Alias": ["`claude-opus`", "`claude-sonnet`", "`gpt55`", "`gpt54`", "`gpt54-mini`", "`codex-gpt53`", "`opencode-go/kimi-k2.6`", "`opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash`", "`opencode-go/qwen3.6-plus`"],
    "Agent": ["Claude Code", "Claude Code", "Codex CLI", "Codex CLI", "Codex CLI", "Codex CLI", "Mini SWE Agent", "Mini SWE Agent", "Mini SWE Agent"],
    "Model ID": ["`claude-opus-4-7`", "`claude-sonnet-4-6`", "`gpt-5.5`", "`gpt-5.4`", "`gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`", "`gpt-5.3-codex`", "`openai-api/opencode-go/kimi-k2.6`", "`openai-api/opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash`", "`openai-api/opencode-go/qwen3.6-plus`"],
    "Provider": ["Anthropic", "Anthropic", "OpenAI", "OpenAI", "OpenAI", "OpenAI", "OpenCode Go", "OpenCode Go", "OpenCode Go"],
})

(
    GT(models)
    .tab_header(title="Supported Models")
    .fmt_markdown(columns=["Alias", "Model ID"])
    .cols_align(align="left")
    .opt_stylize(style=1, color="blue")
)
```

---

## {{< fa chart-bar >}} Cost Comparison

Per 1M tokens. Actual cost per dashboard is typically **$0.01–$0.30** depending on model, prompt complexity, and iterations.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from great_tables import GT, md
import polars as pl

costs = pl.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["`claude-opus`", "`claude-sonnet`", "`gpt55`", "`gpt54`", "`gpt54-mini`", "`codex-gpt53`"],
    "Input": [5.00, 3.00, 5.00, 2.50, 0.75, 1.75],
    "Output": [25.00, 15.00, 30.00, 15.00, 4.50, 14.00],
})

(
    GT(costs)
    .tab_header(
        title="Cost per 1M Tokens",
        subtitle="USD pricing by model",
    )
    .fmt_markdown(columns="Model")
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Input", "Output"], currency="USD")
    .cols_label(Input="Input ($/1M tokens)", Output="Output ($/1M tokens)")
    .cols_align(align="right", columns=["Input", "Output"])
    .cols_align(align="left", columns="Model")
    .data_color(
        columns="Output",
        palette=["#d4edda", "#f8d7da"],
    )
    .opt_stylize(style=1, color="blue")
)
```

---

## {{< fa database >}} Data Inputs

```{python}
#| echo: false
from great_tables import GT, md
import polars as pl

data_inputs = pl.DataFrame({
    "Option": ["Single CSV", "Multiple files"],
    "CLI": ["`--csv-file`", "`--data-file` (repeatable)"],
    "Python API": ["`data_csv`", "`data_files`"],
    "Notes": ["Convenience shorthand", "Any file type"],
})

(
    GT(data_inputs)
    .tab_header(title="Data Input Options")
    .fmt_markdown(columns=["CLI", "Python API"])
    .cols_align(align="left")
    .opt_stylize(style=1, color="blue")
    .tab_source_note(md("If both provide the same filename, `--csv-file` takes precedence."))
)
```

---

## {{< fa list-check >}} Requirements

```{python}
#| echo: false
from great_tables import GT, md
import polars as pl

reqs = pl.DataFrame({
    "Requirement": ["Python", "Docker", "API Key"],
    "Details": ["3.10+", "Running daemon", "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, or OPENCODE_GO_API_KEY"],
})

(
    GT(reqs)
    .tab_header(title="Prerequisites")
    .cols_align(align="left")
    .opt_stylize(style=1, color="blue")
    .tab_source_note(
        md("`export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY='sk-ant-...'`, `export OPENAI_API_KEY='sk-...'`, or `export OPENCODE_GO_API_KEY='sk-...'`")
    )
)
```

---

## {{< fa scale-balanced >}} License

[MIT](LICENSE)
